1: ẹορτὴ τῶν Ἰουδαίων — "a feast of the Jews." Many manuscripts add the article ("the feast"), which would identify this as Passover and anchor the episode in the Johannine chronology of three Passovers. The anarthrous reading ("a feast") is generally preferred by modern editors and leaves the festival unspecified. The Evangelist's purpose is not calendrical but theological: Jesus goes up to Jerusalem for an occasion that will produce a conflict about Sabbath and sonship.
2: Βηθεσδά (Bēthesda) — an Aramaic name meaning "house of mercy" or "house of grace" (bēth ḥesed). Josephus and the Copper Scroll both locate a pool near the Sheep Gate; archaeological excavation has identified a twin-pool complex with five colonnades — four around the perimeter and one crossing between the two basins, matching the Evangelist's detail of five stoai (porticoes). The precision signals an author who knew Jerusalem before 70 CE.
3: The verse lists the disabled: τυφλῶν, χωλῶν, ξηρῶν (blind, lame, withered/paralyzed). The phrase "waiting for the moving of the water" and all of v4 are absent from the oldest manuscripts (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus, P66, P75) and are almost certainly a scribal gloss explaining why the sick gathered there. The Evangelist's original account describes the scene without offering the popular explanation; the healing will come from Jesus alone.
4: This verse does not appear in the earliest manuscripts and is regarded by textual critics as a later addition. It attributes the healing power of the pool to an angel periodically stirring the water — a belief current among those who used it. The Evangelist's account requires no such mechanism; Jesus heals by direct command. The absence from P66, P75, Sinaiticus, and Vaticanus is decisive for its inauthenticity.
5: τριάκοντα καὶ ὀκτὼ ἔτη — thirty-eight years. The number is precise; the Evangelist selects a detail that underscores the severity and duration of the man's condition rather than a round figure. Some have noted that Israel wandered thirty-eight years between Kadesh-Barnea and the Zered crossing (Deuteronomy 2:14), but no such parallel is developed here. The point is helplessness so long-established that healing has become practically unimaginable.
6: θέλεις ὑγιὴς γενέσθαι; — "Do you wish to become well?" The verb θέλω (thelō) carries deliberate volition: Jesus engages the man's will, not merely his medical need. The same word returns in v40 where it indicts those who "are not willing" to come to him. The question frames the entire discourse as a matter of will: divine authority is available, but human receptivity must respond.
7: κύριε, ἄνθρωπον οὐκ ἔχω — "Sir, I have no man." The man answers the question he understood: he interprets Jesus' offer as help into the pool, and his reply reveals complete social isolation. κράβαττον (krabbaton) — a loanword from the Latin grabatus, a common soldier's or poor man's sleeping mat, not a formal bed. The Evangelist's choice signals poverty alongside illness; the mat is all this man owns.
8: ἔγειρε (egeire, present imperative) — "Rise." This verb carries a double register throughout the Fourth Gospel: physical rising and resurrection from death (cf. John 5:21, 25, 28–29; John 2:19–22; John 11:23–24). Its use here anticipates the resurrection discourse of John 5:21–29. Jesus issues three imperatives in sequence — rise, take up your mat, walk — the three together describe full reversal of the man's condition. No formula, no ritual, no intermediary.
9: εὐθέως (eutheōs) — "immediately." The adverb is a standard marker of divine power acting without delay in healing accounts. The appended note — ἦν δὲ σάββατον ("now it was a Sabbath") — is the narrative hinge. The healing is complete before the controversy is revealed. The word order withholds the Sabbath detail until after the miracle, so the reader registers the collision rather than anticipating it.
10: οὐκ ἔξεστίν σοι ἆραι τὸν κράβαττόν σου — "it is not lawful for you to carry your mat." Ἰξεστιν is the language of halakhic ruling — permitted or not permitted under Torah as interpreted. Prohibition against carrying objects on the Sabbath is grounded in Jeremiah 17:21–22 and developed in m. Shabbat John 7:2. The leaders address the healed man, not the healer — policing compliance, not asking about the miracle.
11: ὁ ποιήσας με ὑγιὴ — "the one who made me well." The healed man grounds his obedience in the authority of the one who restored him: the power to give health carries authority to give commands. This echoes the logic running through the whole discourse — the Son acts under the Father's authority (v19), and the healed man acts under the Son's. A chain of delegated authority binds the episode to the theological argument that follows.
12: The leaders' question — τίς ἐστιν ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὁ εἰπών σοι… ("who is the man who told you to...") — edits the miracle out entirely. They quote only the command to carry the mat, ignoring "the one who made me well." The contrast with the healed man's formulation in v11 is deliberate: the leaders cannot see past the halakhic violation to the healing power that produced it.
13: ἐξένευσεν (exeneusen) — "had slipped away" or "had withdrawn quietly." This rare verb (unique in the NT) implies deliberate, unobtrusive departure rather than dramatic exit. The crowd (ὄχλου ὄντος) made withdrawal possible. The healed man genuinely did not know who healed him (v13b) — the anonymity is not evasion; it belongs to the pattern of Jesus acting without seeking personal recognition (cf. v41).
14: μηκέτι ἁμάρτανε — "sin no more" (present imperative of prohibition with μηκέτι = cease an ongoing action). The link between this man's illness and sin is asserted here without being universalized; contrast John 9:2–3, where Jesus explicitly denies that disability is caused by personal sin. Here the connection is individual. χεῖρόν τι σοι γένηται — "something worse may happen to you" — deliberately unspecified; whatever is worse than 38 years of paralysis lies outside the pool of mercy.
15: ἀναγγέλλω (anangelld) — "reported/announced." The verb's prefix (ana-) suggests conveying information up to an authority. The man's motive is not characterized as betrayal by the Evangelist — he may be legitimating his Sabbath-breaking by citing his authority, or simply answering the question he was asked. What follows (v16) is escalated hostility against Jesus, not condemnation of the healed man.
16: ἐποίει (epoiei) — imperfect tense: "was doing" or "kept doing." This is not limited to the Bethesda healing; the imperfect signals habitual practice — Jesus regularly healed on the Sabbath. The leaders' ἐδίωκον ("persecuted/pursued") is also imperfect: an ongoing campaign rather than a single reaction. Two imperfects compress what was an extended conflict into a single summary verse, preparing for the discourse that follows.
17: ὁ πατήρ μου ἕως ἄρτι ἐργάζεται — "My Father works until now/up to this very moment." ἕως ἄρτι asserts continuous divine activity without Sabbath cessation — a claim familiar in Second Temple sources (Philo, Leg. All. 1.18, argues God never ceases to work). ὁ πατήρ μου — "my Father" (not "our Father") — the singular possessive is the theological provocation the leaders immediately identify in v18.
18: ἴσον ἑαυτὸν ποιῶν τῷ θεῷ — "making himself equal with God." ἴσος (isos) means equal in rank, status, or nature — not merely analogous. This is the leaders' own reading of Jesus' claim, not an external theological commentary. They understood "my Father works and I work" as asserting parity, not subordination. The phrase ίδιον πατέρα — "his own Father" (not just "the Father") — is the specific formulation they found intolerable: an exclusive, unique filial relationship.
19: οὐ δύναται ὁ υἱὸς ποιεῖν ἀφ' ἑαυτοῦ οὐδέν — "the Son is not able to do anything from himself." ἀφ' ἑαυτοῦ ("from himself") is the key phrase, repeated in v30: the Son's inability to act independently is not a limitation of power but of relation — he acts always in union with the Father, never in autonomy. The double negative οὐ…οὐδέν is emphatic: nothing whatsoever. The paradox is that this absolute dependence is also absolute identity — "whatever the Father does, the Son does likewise (ὁμοίως)."
20: φιλεῖ γὰρ ὁ πατὴρ τὸν υἱόν — "for the Father loves the Son." The verb is φιλέω (phileō), the word for close relational affection — the warmth of persons who belong together. The Evangelist uses ἀγαπάω (agapaō) for God's love for the world (John 3:16) and the Son (John 3:35); here phileō emphasizes intimacy as the ground of total disclosure. πάντα δείκνυσιν αὐτῷ — "shows him all things" — present tense: continuous, ongoing revelation between Father and Son.
21: ṥσπερ…οὕτως — the structural "as…so" that undergirds the Son's equality throughout this discourse. ζωοποιεῖ (zōopoiei) — "gives life" — a compound of zōē (life) + poieō (make); it is used in the LXX and NT for the divine prerogative of creating and restoring life (cf. Romans 4:17; 1 Cor John 15:22). That the Son gives life "to whom he wills" (οὓς θέλει) signals sovereign discretion — the same thelō that reappears in John 5:39–40.
22: κρίσιν πᾶσαν δέδωκεν τῷ υἱῷ — "has committed all judgment to the Son." The perfect δέδωκεν marks a completed, permanent delegation. Πᾶσαν — "all" — is absolute; no residual divine judgment exists outside the Son. The verse moves from life-giving (v21) to judgment as the two ultimate divine acts: to create and to adjudicate. Both are now the Son's.
23: ἵνα πάντες τιμῶσιν τὸν υἱόν — "so that all may honor the Son." The purpose clause (ἵνα) reveals why judgment was delegated: equal honor. Τιμάω in a Mediterranean honor-shame context is not sentimental respect but public acknowledgment of a person's status. The negative is blunt: ὁ μὴ τιμῶν τὸν υἱὸν οὐ τιμᾶ τὸν πατέρα — "the one not honoring the Son does not honor the Father." There is no route around the Son to the Father.
24: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν — the double "truly truly" (unique to the Fourth Gospel) introduces a claim of singular weight. μεταβέβηκεν ἐκ τοῦ θανάτου εἰς τὴν ζωήν — "has crossed over from death into life." Metabebēken is perfect tense: a completed transfer with permanent result. The crossing has already happened for the one who hears and believes — not a future event but a present accomplished state. εἰς κρίσιν οὐκ ἔρχεται — "does not come into judgment" — the eschatological verdict has already been resolved for the believer.
25: ἔρχεται ṥρα καὶ νῦν ἐστιν — "an hour is coming and now is." The phrase "and now is" is critical and absent from v28. The spiritual resurrection Jesus describes here is not only future but present: the spiritually dead already hear the Son's voice and live. This is realized eschatology — the future hour has broken into the present. Οἱ νεκροί here are the spiritually dead; οἱ ἐν τοῖς μνημείοις (v28) are the physically buried. The Evangelist distinguishes carefully between the two resurrections.
26: ζωὴν ἐν ἑαυτῷ — "life in himself." This is the uniquely divine attribute of self-existent life — aseitas, life whose source is in itself and depends on nothing outside. The Father has this intrinsically; the Son has it by the Father's grant (ἔδωκεν, aorist: a definite act of giving). The tension between the derivative reception (he was granted it) and the self-existent character (once given, it is life-in-himself) is deliberate: it preserves both the Son's eternal equality with the Father and his relational distinction within the Godhead.
27: υἱὸς ἀνθρώπου — strikingly, this phrase appears without the article — "a son of man" rather than "the Son of Man." The anarthrous form invokes the Danielic figure (Daniel 7:13) who receives dominion and judgment from the Ancient of Days, while emphasizing the full humanity of the judge: authority to execute judgment (κρίσιν ποιεῖν) belongs to him because he is human, not only because he is divine. The double nature is the ground of judicial authority.
28: μὴ θαυμάζετε τοῦτο — "do not be amazed at this." The command to stop marveling prepares for a still greater claim. Critically, this verse says ἔρχεται ṥρα ("an hour is coming") without "and now is" — distinguishing physical resurrection (John 5:28–29) from spiritual quickening (v25). οἱ ἐν τοῖς μνημείοις — "those in the tombs" — is the language of physical burial. The shift from John 5:24–27 (present spiritual reality) to John 5:28–29 (future physical event) is the Evangelist's two-register structure for the resurrection theme.
29: ἀνάστασιν ζωῆς…ἀνάστασιν κρίσεως — "resurrection of life…resurrection of judgment." The phrase echoes Daniel 12:2 ("some to everlasting life and some to shame and everlasting contempt"). Κρίσεως here means "judgment" — the KJV "damnation" is too loaded; it is the verdict of the divine court, which for these will be adverse. ἀγαθά / φαῦλα — "good/evil (things practiced)" — φαῦλος (phaulos) means base or worthless rather than the more dramatic kakos; the contrast is between deeds that are genuinely good and deeds that are fundamentally hollow.
30: οὐ δύναμαι ἐγὼ ποιεῖν ἀπ' ἐμαυτοῦ οὐδέν — "I am not able to do anything from myself." The formula from v19 returns in the first person, now applied specifically to judgment. καθὼς ἀκούω κρίνω — "as I hear, I judge" — the Son's judgment is derivative: he hears and renders the Father's verdict. Δικαία — "just" — the judgment is just precisely because it has no personal agenda; οὐ ζητῶ τὸ θέλημα τὸ ἐμόν — "I do not seek my own will" — justice is defined as the subordination of self-will to the Father's.
31: ἡ μαρτυρία μου οὐκ ἔστιν ἀληθής — "my testimony is not true." This is not a denial of truthfulness but an invocation of the legal principle: under Torah, a single witness cannot validate his own claim (Deuteronomy 17:6; 19:15 requires two or three witnesses). Jesus concedes the formal objection before presenting four witnesses in sequence (the Father, John, the works, the scriptures). The verse is transitional: it acknowledges the rule before demolishing the objection.
32: ἄλλος ἐστιν ὁ μαρτυρῶν περὶ ἐμοῦ — "there is another who testifies about me." Ἄλλος (allos) means another of the same kind — a divine witness rather than a human one. The verse refers to the Father (v37 makes this explicit), not yet to John the Baptist. οἶδα ὅτι ἀληθής ἐστιν — "I know that his testimony is true" — Jesus affirms the Father's testimony without waiting for the audience to accept it; the claim is declarative, not defensive.
33: ὑμεῖς ἀπεσταλκατε πρὸς Ἰωάννην — "you have sent to John" (perfect: the mission stands completed). Both verbs in this verse are perfect tense — apestalikate (you have sent) and mem arturēken (he has borne witness) — marking completed, abiding actions. Jesus appeals to a testimony they themselves solicited: the delegation to John (John 1:19–28) was their own initiative, and John's answer is part of the record they cannot erase.
34: ἐγὼ δὲ οὐ παρὰ ἀνθρώπου τὴν μαρτυρίαν λαμβάνω — "but I do not receive the testimony from man." Jesus does not rest his case on human witness, however reliable. The verb λαμβάνω here means "accept as the primary basis." His purpose in citing John's testimony is explicitly pastoral: ἵνα ὑμεῖς σωθῆτε — "that you might be saved" — an appeal to what they already acknowledged.
35: ἐκεῖνος ἦν ὁ λύχνος — "he was the lamp." Λύχνος (lychnos, lamp) is carefully distinguished from φῶς (phōs, light) — John 1:8 says John was not the light. A lamp is derivative: it is lit from another source and gives off borrowed light. ὁ καιόμενος καὶ φαίνων — "the one burning and shining" — two participles: the lamp burns (consumes itself) while shining. ἀγαλλιαθῆναι πρὸς ṥραν — "to rejoice for a season" — ἀγαλλιάω is ecstatic, exultant joy; they basked in John's excitement briefly but did not follow his witness to its conclusion.
36: ἐγὼ δὲ ἔχω τὴν μαρτυρίαν μείζω τοῦ Ἰωάννου — "I have the testimony greater than John's." The comparative μείζω does not diminish John but subordinates him within a hierarchy of witnesses. τὰ ἔργα ἃ ἔδωκέν μοι ὁ πατὴρ ἵνα τελειώσω αὐτά — "the works the Father gave me to complete (teleioō)." The verb τελειόω (bring to completion/perfection) reappears in the Passion narrative ("it is finished," John 19:30), linking these works here to their culminating act.
37: ὁ πέμψας με πατὴρ ἐκεῖνος μεμαρτύρηκεν περὶ ἐμοῦ — "the Father who sent me, that one has borne witness (perfect) about me." The perfect mem arturēken marks completed, standing testimony. οὔτε φωνὴν αὐτοῦ πώποτε ἀκηκόατε οὔτε εἶδος αὐτοῦ ὑράκατε — "you have never heard his voice nor seen his form." The accusation is not that the Father has withheld testimony, but that they lack the faculty to receive it — their capacity for hearing God has not been formed.
38: τὸν λόγον αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔχετε ἐν ὑμῖν μένοντα — "you do not have his word abiding (menō) in you." Mένω (abide, remain, take up residence) is one of the Evangelist's signature verbs (cf. John 15:4–10; 1 John 2:14). The word is not merely heard or stored; it must take up permanent residence and shape the person from within. Their failure is not lack of Scripture but lack of the word's active indwelling — the letter without the life.
39: ἐρευνᾶτε τὰς γραφάς — most likely indicative: "you search the Scriptures" (describing their practice, not commanding it). ὅτι ὑμεῖς δοκεῖτε ἐν αὐταῖς ζωὴν αἰώνιον ἔχειν — "because you think that in them you have eternal life." Δοκεῖτε marks this as their assumption, not endorsement by Jesus. The Scriptures are not wrong; the assumption that possessing the text equals possessing the life is wrong. ἐκεῖναί εἰσιν αἱ μαρτυροῦσαι περὶ ἐμοῦ — "these are the ones testifying about me" — the Scriptures' purpose is witness, not self-contained life-delivery.
40: οὐ θέλετε ἐλθεῖν πρός με — "you are not willing to come to me." The obstacle is named as θέλω (will), not capacity. This is the same verb Jesus used in v6 ("do you wish to be made well?"). Those who are unwilling to come reproduce the spiritual condition of the man who had been 38 years unable to reach the water: both suffer from a failure of the right kind of wanting. The verse is the moral center of the discourse.
41: δόξαν παρὰ ἀνθρώπων οὐ λαμβάνω — "I do not receive glory from men." Δόξα (glory) here is the public honor and acknowledgment that constitute the social currency of the ancient honor-shame world. Jesus is not indifferent to truth; he is structurally indifferent to human opinion as a basis for his identity or mission. This detachment makes his testimony trustworthy and his diagnosis of them (v42) credible: he is not competing with them for the same currency.
42: τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ θεοῦ οὐκ ἔχετε ἐν ἑαυτοῖς — "you do not have the love of God in yourselves." Τὴν ἀγάπην τοῦ θεοῦ is an objective genitive: love for God, not God's love for them. The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4–5) demanded total love for God as the founding covenant commandment. Jesus says they lack this — a withering charge given that the Pharisees wore the Shema on their foreheads in phylacteries. ἐγνωκα ὑμᾶς — perfect tense: "I have come to know you, and this is my settled knowledge."
43: ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρός μου — "in the name of my Father." To come in someone's name is to act as their authorized representative, under their authority and on their behalf. Jesus comes representing the Father; his credentials are the Father's commission. That they refuse him while ready to accept an autonomous claimant (en tō onomati tō idiō, "in his own name") reveals an inversion: they distrust authenticated authority and prefer self-assertion.
44: δόξαν παρ' ἀλλήλων λαμβάνοντες — "receiving glory from one another." The present participle is causal: the ongoing exchange of human honor among themselves is why they cannot believe. τὴν δόξαν τὴν παρὰ τοῦ μόνου θεοῦ οὐ ζητεῖτε — "you do not seek the glory that comes from the only God." Τοῦ μόνου θεοῦ — "the only God" — the uniqueness of God exposes the absurdity of preferring human validation. The social economy they inhabit is a closed system that excludes God.
45: ἔστιν ὁ κατηγορῶν ὑμῶν Μωϋσῆς — "there is the one accusing you — Moses." Κατηγορέω is legal terminology (to bring charges in court). The reversal is sharp: Moses, the mediator of Torah on whom they stake their hope (ἠλπίκατε, perfect: "on whom you have set your hope"), becomes the prosecutor. The perfect tense marks settled, established trust — but that trust is misplaced if Moses' writings lead to Jesus and they refuse to follow.
46: περὶ γὰρ ἐμοῦ ἐκεῖνος ἔγραψεν — "for concerning me, that one wrote." The emphatic pronoun ἐκεῖνος ("that one") points to Moses as a specific historical figure and named authority. The claim is comprehensive: Moses wrote about me — not incidentally, but as the forward-pointing content of his writing. No particular Mosaic texts are itemized; the claim is global. Jesus places himself as the telos of the entire Pentateuch.
47: τοῖς ἐκείνου γράμμασιν…τοῖς ἐμοῖς ῥήμασιν — "his writings…my words." Γράμματα (grammata) are letters, written texts — the fixed, authoritative Mosaic corpus. Ẕήματα (rhēmata) are spoken utterances. The contrast is not written vs. oral per se, but the established text they already possess and revere vs. the present voice speaking to them now. If they cannot read the Christ-testimony in what they already have, they have no faculty left for receiving the direct word of the one Moses pointed toward.
